title: 9 Grüner Veltliners That’ll Have You Swooning
summary:
Whether you prefer it rich and creamy or sharp and high-acid, Grüner Veltliner can be whatever you want it to be.

The post 9 Grüner Veltliners That’ll Have You Swooning appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Grüner Veltliner is Austria’s ultimate viticultural chameleon. As the country’s most widely planted varietal, it thrives across diverse locations—from cool, elevated sites to sheltered slopes—and soils ranging from gravel to fertile loess. This adaptability allows it to succeed in a broad spectrum of styles, influenced heavily by winemaking decisions like skin-contact and oak and lees aging.

In its most approachable form, Grüner is light-bodied, zippy, and refreshing, with a hint of natural white pepper. This spicy edge comes from rotundone, a compound found in the skins that is also prevalent in Syrah.

The grape’s range runs much deeper, though. In the Wachau, Kremstal, and Kamptal, harvests produce taut, mineral-rich wines built to age, while loess-heavy Wagram can yield rounder, creamier expressions. These regions are certainly worthy of their renown, but to Aleks Zecevic, a Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large who reviews wines from Germany, Austria, and sections of France, there is one region that’s often overlooked in Grüner Veltliner coverage. “I think Burgenland is producing the most exciting and underrated expressions,” he says, “… especially when grown in slightly cooler sites on limestone or schist.”

But Grüner’s success isn’t confined to just Austria. The varietal has found real momentum in areas like the United States’ West Coast and Italy’s Alto Adige. “There have been exciting variations of Grüner Veltliner being grown all over Northern California,” says Tonya Pitts, a WE Writer-at-Large who reviews wines from the Golden State. She notes that domestic versions lean toward more aromatic, less mineral-driven profiles than their Austrian counterparts.

Meanwhile, in Alto Adige, the grape takes on a distinctly alpine character. “Where Austrian Grüners can have weight, GVs from Alto Adige are crystalline, full of verve and lightning,” says Jeff Porter, WE Writer-at-Large and Northern Italian wine reviewer. “Not better, just different.” 

Whether you prefer it rich and creamy or sharp and high-acid, Grüner can be whatever you want—if you know what to look for. Read on for our favorite bottles of this versatile varietal.

Classic and Mineral-Rich Grüners from Austria

Weingut Sommer 2022 Halser Grüner Veltliner (Leithaberg)

A masterful Grüner, showing flinty minerality and lifted white raspberry aromatics. Goldenberry and pear flavors mingle seamlessly, with a silky, full-bodied texture and remarkable intensity. The finish is precise, layered, and lingering, showcasing the vineyard’s signature elegance. 94 Points — Aleks Zecevic

$40 Leon & Son

Nittnaus Anita und Hans 2022 Elektra (Weinland Österreich)

The essence of limestone in a glass, this is very expressive, with a flinty reduction that adds to the charm of persimmon, freshly squeezed lime and kumquat notes at the core. Streaks of sea salt and cardamom add to the complexity. However, its charm doesn’t stop with the flavor, as the texture is seductive, caressing the palate, but also showing a slight grip. The long finish is multilayered and inviting. Editors’ Choice. 95 Points — A.Z.

$33 Mandara Wine

Weingut Bernhard Ott 2023 Ried Spiegel 1ÖTW Grüner Veltliner (Wagram)

Dry and layered, with a core of yellow apple and goldberry complemented by subtle baking spice, savory minerality, and herbal nuances. The palate is precise and structured, showing depth and complexity, with a long, harmonious finish. Elegant and expressive, a refined example of Spiegel’s site character. 92 Points — A.Z.

$57 Empire Wine

Aromatic West Coast Grüners

Luna Hart 2024 Spear Vineyard Grüner Veltliner (Sta. Rita Hills)

This grape brings both beauty and boldness to the table, in this case beginning with a golden hue and aromas of lemon peel and grapefruit flesh on the nose. The scintillating mouthfeel fully envelops the palate, where zesty green flavors of citrus, apple and grass would pair well with sharp salads. 93 Points — Matt Kettmann

$41 Luna Hart

Illahe 2024 Estate Grüner Veltliner (Mount Pisgah)

There is a purity of tropical fruit here that is irresistible. Aged in a combination of 25% Acacia barrels and 75% stainless steel, this estate Grüner Veltliner offers a grassy perfume of of passion fruit and guava aromas. Acidity that edges up to brisk territory then spotlights flavors like papaya and guava, with a trace amount of white pepper for good measure. The wine is an islands getaway in a glass that feels lively and crisp on the palate. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — Michael Alberty

$29 Taylor’s Wine Shop

Cadre 2024 Band of Stones Grüner Veltliner (San Luis Obispo Coast)

Subtle aromas of underripe apricot and honeydew show on the nose of this bottling. There’s a tingly texture to the palate, where citrus and light grass flavors align. 92 Points — M.K.

$30 Cadre

Electric Grüners from Italy

Kuenhof 2023 (Alto Adige)

This top-notch Grüner Veltliner exudes a lively spirit, showcasing flecks of green in the glass. Aromas of kaffir lime, lemon blossom, fresh bay leaf, pea shoots, and crisp green apple create a vivid aromatic profile. The palate mirrors these vibrant notes with laser-like precision and brightness. It’s drinking superbly now but has the potential to age gracefully. A stellar example of the grape. Drink Now – 2040. Cellar Selection. 95 Points — Jeff Porter

$46 Yiannis

Cantina Valle Isarco 2023 Grüner Veltliner (Alto Adige)

This enchanting Veltliner from Alto Adige captivates with its lifted, pretty floral aromas. Light and fresh, it bursts with vibrant green apple, pear, and lime notes that intertwine with a delightful peppery spiciness. The wine’s great structure and lively acidity showcase the grape’s affinity for the Isarco Valley’s sunny days and cool nights. A charming and refreshing gem, that will also age. Drink Now – 2035. Best Buy. 93 Points — J.P.

$20 The Wine Shop and Wine Bar

Abbazia di Novacella 2024 Grüner Veltliner (Alto Adige Valle Isarco)

Nine centuries of winemaking wisdom show in every glass, but this vintage brings modern swagger. Bitter orange dances with snap pea freshness while white pepper adds mountain spice. The palate proves why these monk-tended slopes matter, where citrus pith meets mineral precision before a savory finish that makes you contemplate life’s bigger questions. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — J.P.

$28 Eataly Vino

title: Do Pickles Actually Belong in Your Sauvignon Blanc?
summary:
Are cornichons, dill pickles, or bread and butter pickles good in wine? We did the research so you don’t have to.

The post Do Pickles Actually Belong in Your Sauvignon Blanc? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Another year, another wacky wine trend. Sigh.

If you are active on the wine and food corner of social media, you may have seen a recent TikTok pronouncing “this is your sign to put pickles in your Chicken Wine.” In the short video, the creator adds slices of frozen pickles to a wine glass along with ice, La Vieille Ferme’s white Rhône Valley blend, and a splash of pickle juice. She stirs her glass, takes a sip, and gives a knowing chef’s kiss. 

Over a million views later, the trend has taken off—to mixed reviews. Brave testers are calling the concoction everything from “genius” to “not bad” to “a little off.” 

“I am not one to disrespect a glass of wine,” another creator states, but, she wonders, is it really disrespectful if it involves pickles? Apparently, it’s time to find out.

Last year, when the jalapeño Sauvignon Blanc trend was spreading like wildfire, I indulged my curiosity (much to the dismay of my wine shop coworkers). Even worse? I didn’t hate it. The peppery heat added a twisted layer of intrigue to my otherwise simple glass. While I haven’t added jalapeños to my Sauvy B since then, I must admit that I felt the smallest hint of excitement when the pickle wine trend came across my feed. 

With the popularity of dirty martinis paving the way for pickle martinis and dirty spritzes, this felt like an idea that just might work, especially by embracing the wine cocktail vibes and filling the glass with ice. (The visual of pickles floating in a glass of wine is admittedly less than appetizing.) So, with nothing stopping me but a quick run to the bottle shop and grocery store, I decided to indulge my curiosity once again.

Choosing My Pickles and Sauvignon Blanc

For this experiment, I chose an organic Sauvignon Blanc from Domaine Tardieux-Gal in the Loire Valley. Hailing from eastern Touraine, this wine is crisp and mineral with just a whisper of lemon, herbs, and spice. Its simplicity felt like a good place to start. 

At the grocery store, as jars upon jars of different pickles stared back at me like a challenge, I decided to make things a little more interesting. With a heavy bag full of cornichons, bread and butter pickles, and dill pickles in tow, I returned home and got to work.

Once my pickles were frozen and my wine was chilled, I lined up the test subjects and dove into the taste test. To each glass, I added a sprinkling of frozen pickle slices, a small splash of the respective pickle juice, and ice before filling with the wine.

Up First: Classic Cornichons

Upon my first whiff of the cornichons, my excitement plummeted. The smell was offputting, to say the least. On their own, the cornichon’s pickle juice is nicely briny and tart, while the wine is smooth, earthy, and citrusy. When combined, these aromas manifested as something much more reminiscent of low tide at the ocean. 

I mentally prepared myself and took my first sip. I’m sad to report that the flavors on the palate mirrored those on the nose—and not in a good way. The pickles woefully overpowered the wine and resulted in a sip that tasted like acidic seawater mixed with onions and herbs. Yuck.

What About Dill Pickles?

Undeterred but slightly more wary, I moved on to the dill pickles, which have a frighteningly heavier hand of garlic and, well, dill. Again, I braced my senses and took a sniff. This time, the aroma was predictably garlicky but not totally unpleasant. However, it completely masked any hint of the wine and honestly just made me hungry for a pickle. 

The palate was less redeemable. The dill pickle’s vegetal qualities shone through in a way that felt incompatible with the wine. If you’re really committed to making this work, I’d recommend a sip of wine followed by a (separate!) bite of dill pickle. Alternatively, you might try adding a few slices of plain cucumber. While the wine and brine were at war, the pickle’s vegetal earthiness was not an entirely unwelcome addition, so I could see how a simple cucumber could do the trick.

Last But Not Least: Bread and Butter Pickles

By the time I got to the bread and butter pickles, the pairing I was the most hopeful about, I was feeling defeated. Nevertheless, I powered on and was relieved to find that the nose of the final experiment was milder. It was lightly tart and a little salty, but the wine’s smooth minerality persisted. Would the third time be the charm? 

Yes and no. The mellow acidity and salinity of the pickles were inoffensive alongside the citrusy, herbal Sauvignon Blanc, but the grassy spices were a little much for my tastes. If you’re a diehard pickle lover, you might be able to make this one work. Otherwise, I’d recommend steering clear of pickles in your wine. 

Adding pickles to wine is only the latest trend of unexpected wine mix-ins, but it’s unlikely to be the last. So, where will we draw the line? Frozen berries in chilled reds? Orange slices in orange wine? Slabs of butter in Chardonnay?? I’m not sure. All I know is that I’ll be first in line when the next wine trend comes around, and until then, I’ll be keeping pickles far away from my wine.

title: Does Light Drinking Lower Cancer Risk? Depends Who You Ask
summary:
A recently released study found light drinkers had lower rates of cancer mortality. By the time it made the media rounds, another report came to a different conclusion.

The post Does Light Drinking Lower Cancer Risk? Depends Who You Ask appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
A recent study sheds new insights on the relationship between alcohol and health. Published in the Journal of General Internal Medicine this May, the report found no link between moderate alcohol consumption and cancer mortality. Furthermore, the study, led by Laura C. Pinheiro PhD (a cancer researcher at Cornell) and sponsored by the National Institutes of Health and the Department of Health & Human Service, noted that light drinkers had lower rates of cancer mortality. 

Good news for those who want to enjoy an evening glass guilt free, right? 

Of course, those findings were immediately met with more bad news. Soon after Pinheiro’s findings started making the media rounds last week, another report commissioned under the Biden Administration—that had been sidelined by the Trump administration—found that even low levels of alcohol consumption can greatly increase health risks. So, what gives?

The Glass Is Half Full

Let’s start with Pinheiro’s research. Data was pulled from the REGARDS cohort study, a comprehensive data set that kicked off between 2003 and 2007 by following a sample of 26,694 American adults. It analyzed a wide group of participants, who were asked about their alcohol consumption, and controlled for other variables, like how much folks smoked and if they had any outstanding health conditions. Deaths, and causes of deaths, were flagged using medical records.

The report suggested light drinkers—folks who consume less than three drinks per week—have a decreased risk of cancer mortality (6.42 per 1,000 people) compared to abstainers (7.6 per 1,000). It found no association between moderate drinking—seven per week for women and 14 for men—and cancer mortality.

The highest rate of cancer deaths were found in heavy drinkers (10.6 per 1,000 persons), women who consume more than seven drinks per week and men who have more than 14. 

Just over 2,300 of the nearly 27,000 participants died of cancer. The report does not distinguish between various forms of cancer (i.e., breast, lung, etc.) and their connection with alcohol, but it does factor in external variables: socio-economic status, medical conditions, demographics, physical inactivity, obesity, and cigarette use. 

Those external factors can play a huge role in the relationship between alcohol and health. 

​”People who can afford moderate drinking often have better access to healthcare nutrition and stress management which can mask some of the negative effects in population studies,” says Dr. Greg J. Marchand, a master surgeon at the not-for-profit Marchand Institute. ​”And, patients who exercise regularly eat balanced meals and maintain strong social connections usually handle moderate alcohol better than those who do not.” 

This is why the report’s findings encouraged “cancer prevention strategies to step away from isolating single health behaviors and consider holistic perspectives of an individual’s lifestyle including physical activity, smoking, diet, and alcohol consumption.”

The Asterixes and Important Considerations

Does this mean as you’re keeping consumption to light-to-moderate, there’s nothing to worry about?

The issue is, this new report piles onto the ever-evolving body of data on the relationship between alcohol consumption and health.

Without a doubt, all studies underline that heavy and binge drinking—starting at more than one glass per day for women and two for men—greatly increases your risks of cardiovascular disease and many different types of cancer. 

But whether a few glasses per week is going to move the needle is not as clear cut. Some reports, like the recent findings reported in the Journal of General Internal Medicine, find benefits, or a general lack of serious risks, in conscious consumption.

Others find any alcohol—an occasional glass, a Friday martini—will increase risks. In 2023, the World Health Organization published a statement in the Lancet that there is no safe amount of alcohol consumption.

The most recent study to be released, “Alcohol Intake and Health Study: No Protective Effect at Low Levels, With Mortality Increasing to 1 in 25 at 14 Drinks Per Week,” hot on the heels of the Journal of General Internal Medicine report, added to that scary slate. The findings were jarring for moderate drinkers —according to the report, a couple of drinks per day can increase health risks. 

It found lifetime risk of dying from alcohol-related causes, including injuries and motor vehicle accidents, increased from a relatively small 1 in 1,000 for one drink a day to concerning 1 in 25 for two drinks a day. While it did find “small protective associations at up to 3 drinks per week for females and males,” it also said “they were not statistically significant.”

As a result, the report suggested tightening the U.S. Dietary Guidelines to recommend no more than one drink per day for both men and women, as opposed to the former one drink per day for women and two for men. Executed by Sinead George and colleagues at the University of Toronto, it was one of two commissioned to inform the update of the U.S. Dietary Guidelines. 

Last week, it was brought to light that the report had been covered up by the Trump administration, with congressional guidance not to publish it. The commissioning official, Robert M. Vincent was fired, which he claimed was because the report was at odds with commercial interest. 

The second report that informed the guidelines, conducted by the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine, landed on a different conclusion—one drink a day for women and two for men is healthier than not drinking at all.

In the end, the administration removed the alcohol serving recommendations from the US Dietary Guidelines.

“The new dietary guidelines kept it very simple and straightforward in the consumer-facing materials of simply saying drinking less is better for your heath,” says Dr. Amanda Berger, a senior vice president of science and research for the alcohol trade association the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States. “It’s common sense.”

title: The Best Wines to Bring for Every Kind of Picnic
summary:
From blouges on the beach to Styrian Morillon on a mountain, here are the best bottles for every kind of picnic.

The post The Best Wines to Bring for Every Kind of Picnic appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
It’s Saturday morning, the sun is high in the sky, and a beautiful 80 degree day is about to unfold. Naturally, you decide to hit the park with some friends. You stop by your local market for a selection of cheeses, crackers, spreads, dips, fruits, nuts, and sweets before making a patch of grass your home for the rest of the day.

If not the park, then maybe it’s a family beach day. After shoving beach chairs and towels in the trunk and remembering to apply sunscreen, it’s time to hit the sand. When stomachs start to grumble, there’s nothing more life-affirming than a trusty beach sandwich and a bag of chips. We just hope there’s a snack shack nearby for a frosty treat later on. 

But wait—between bites of soft brie and crunchy chips, you’re bound to get thirsty, and no amount of water can quench this craving. This is a job for wine.

Whether by the ocean or up a mountain, the right bottle of wine can help upgrade any picnic, no matter the setting. We asked WE Writers-at-Large Aleks Zecevic, Cody Wexler, and Tom R. Capo to break down their favorite bottles for every picnic locale.

Buy Coastal Bottlings for the Boat

A good boat picnic needs fresh seafood, so our reviewers recommend choosing wines that are up to the pairing challenge.

“There is no better place to pair seafood and rosé than when you’re literally on the ocean,” Wexler says, pointing to Provence as the ideal choice. “A lot of the vineyards in Provence are right next to the beach,” he says, “and the wines have a really special salinity.”

If rosé doesn’t fit your mood, Capo recommends looking to other coastal regions. “A proper boat picnic should always involve fresh seafood and crisp, chilled whites,” he says. “I love a Vermentino from coastal Italy to play into that salty sea air.”

Provence Rosé

Octopoda 2024 Rosé (Côtes de Provence)

This wine starts out a little shy but has a pleasant peach bubblegum nose. This rosé is coats your palate with a lingering heat and has the structure and acidity for a great food wine. Pair with a cheese plate. 91 Points — Cody Wexler

$14 Applejack

Commanderie de Peyrassol 2024 Les Commandeurs Rosé (Côtes de Provence)

Classic Provence nose full of peach aromas with swirling minerality. The palate is fruit forward and leans into its peachiness with a slight candied note. 90 Points — C.W.

$25 VinoNueva

Vermentino

Tua Rita 2024 Perlato del Bosco Vermentino (Vermentino di Toscana)

Lemon, lime, grapefruit, fresh cut grass and wild flowers on the nose open up for yellow apple, honey, and nuts on the palate. These notes plus the full mouthfeel make for an incredible richness, but a vein of saline cuts through and citrus lifts up the palate. This is Vermentino living its best life right now. 93 Points — Danielle Callegari

$25 The Wine Wave

Collemassari 2024 Melacce Vermentino (Maremma Toscana)

The nose offers salty, flinty aromas of lime, crushed rocks and raw almonds, and the palate opens with salt that accentuates more almond and citrus notes, before some pineapple and tropical fruit flavors sneak out for balance. Acid dances around the mouth. Best Buy. 90 Points — D.C.

$19 Wine.com

Refreshing Wines for the Beach

“I love bringing a chilled blouge to the beach,” Zecevic says, “because it’s ok if it gets slightly warm.” A fancy term for wines made from both red and white grapes, he specifically suggests finding a blouge where the grapes are co-fermented. These wines are often lighter, chillable reds where the addition of white grapes lends a distinct lift. As such, they’re easily enjoyed at a range of temperatures—an important factor when settling in for a long beach day. “They pair well with most foods you eat at the beach,” Zecevic adds.

While delicious, blouges can be hard to track down, but there are plenty of good options that are a little more straightforward. “Vinho Verdes and Txakolinas are refreshing and pair with a wonderfully wide range of foods,” Capo says. “Always go for simplicity and ease when you’re hanging in the sand all day.”

Co-Fermented Blouge

Scar of the Sea 2021 Bassi Vineyard Co-Ferment Red (San Luis Obispo County)

This is a fascinating fermented blend of 66% Pinot Gris and 34% Pinot Noir, showing a purple-ish Pinot color in the glass and hitting the nose with snappy raspberry, pomegranate and herb aromas. The palate is extremely textural, with white pepper, dried herb and more bright red-fruit flavors. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — Matt Kettmann

$38 Vail Fine Wines

Paraduxx 2021 Winemaker’s Series Co-Ferment Red (Napa Valley)

A fresh and enticing blend of Syrah, Viognier and Grenache fermented together lifts floral aromatics from the glass, followed by juicy fruits, and a long savory finish. Pleasingly mouthwatering and delicious. Will do as well with a pizza as it will for a sit-down meal of autumn delights. 93 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

$104 Only Dram

Funckenhausen 2024 Original Cofermented Series Malbec (Mendoza)

Fruit-forward aromas define the nose of this Malbec co-fermented with a hint of Riesling. The palate highlights hibiscus and pomegranate, with floral notes, balanced by moderate freshness and gentle tannins. 89 Points — Jesica Vargas

$16 Empire Wine

Vinho Verde

Soalheiro 2024 Clássico Alvarinho (Vinho Verde Monção e Melgaço)

This fresh wine from the Monção and Melgaço region in the north of Vinho Verde is full of young white fruit flavors and a citrus aroma. The Alvarinho brings a creamy texture that will age further. Drink from 2026. 91 Points — Roger Voss

$20 Wine.com

João Portugal Ramos 2024 Alvarinho (Vinho Verde)

A ripe, creamy and spicy wine with green fruits that are freshened by citrus acidity. The wine has a pink grapefruit aroma and a vibrant, youthful texture. From the Monção and Melgaço region, flanked by mountains in farthest northern Portugal, the wine will be ready from 2026. Best Buy. 90 Points — R.V.

$23 Wine.com

Txakolina

Gaintza 2024 Txakolina Roses Hondarrabi Beltza-Hondarrabi Zuri (Getariako Txakolina)

Glittering salmon-pink in the glass, with aromatics of alpine strawberry zested with lemon and a sprinkle of sea salt. The palate stays refreshing and light while capturing a level of flavor not always common to the style. Delicious. 92 Points — E.C.B.

$30 Flatiron Wines

Lasalde Elkartea 2021 Hondarrabi Beltza-Hondarrabi Zuri (Getariako Txakolina)

Aromas of white peach and orange blossom set the stage for Granny Smith apple, slivered almond, Turkish delight and dried-sage flavors. Well textured and with good acidity, this is an enjoyable pour. 91 Points — Mike DeSimone

$23 Wide Eyed Wines

Pack Dry Lambrusco for the Park

Park picnics are all about ease—the best don’t require a lot of planning and can be spontaneously thrown together at the last minute. These kinds of gatherings call for wines that are equally low maintenance.

“There is no better wine for park picnics than a dry Lambrusco,” Zecevic says, explaining that these bubbles are just downright refreshing. Plus, they don’t require a corkscrew and even taste great out of low-quality, plastic cups!

Dry Lambrusco

Albinea Canali NV Ottocentonero Lambrusco Lambrusco Grasparossa (Emilia)

Deep and serious for Lambrusco, showing concentrated black fruits and dried herbs with a savory edge. The palate balances ripe blackberry and plum with hints of orange peel and purple flowers. Sturdy tannins provide structure uncommon in the category. Perfect with Emilian classics like cotechino or, venturing further, Korean BBQ beef. 90 Points — Jeff Porter

$20 Little West

Lucci NV Lambrusco Secco Lambrusco (Reggiano)

Like finding plum concentrate that somehow got even darker, while cracked pepper and salumi notes start planning the menu. The palate keeps that food-friendly focus, juicy dark fruits and savory depth making it clear this wine was built for Memphis dry-rub ribs and smoky baked beans. Best Buy. 91 Points — J.P.

$21 Total Wine

Bring Salty Wine on a Hike

No hike is complete without a satisfying picnic to look forward to as you trek to the top. Add some good wine to the mix, and we’d never leave the mountain. 

In Zecevic’s opinion, a salty white is the perfect match for the crisp mountain air. “A Savagnin from the Jura or a Morillon [also known as Chardonnay] from Styria would be invigorating,” he says. These wines tend to have a balance of savoriness, minerality, and salinity that make them particularly delightful after a long hike. If you really want to get in the spirit, Zecevic suggests packing a nice mountain cheese in your picnic basket.

Jura Savagnin

Rijckaert 2022 Grand Elevage Vieilles Vignes (Arbois)

From old vines, this Savagnin is fruity while also dense. It has a bite of almonds and zesty acidity. That is allied to young fruitiness so the wine needs to age further. Drink from 2025. 91 Points — R.V.

$42 Hart & Cru

Domaine Dugois 2023 Auréoline Savagnin (Arbois)

Slightly oxidative, showing light nutty aromas and flavors alongside ripe overquince and orange peel. The palate is lively and vibrant, with bright acidity and a clean, focused finish. Balanced and intriguing, with subtle complexity and a distinctive character. 90 Points — Aleks Zecevic

$31 Liner & Elsen

Styrian Morillon

Lackner Tinnacher 2022 Ried Eckberg Chardonnay (Südsteiermark)

This shows notes of flint and hominy upfront, followed by a juicy core which makes your mouth water, displaying clementine, white raspberry and nectarine flavors. It is texturally inviting, with a silky smooth impression which marks the long finish. 91 Points — A.Z.

$30 Beverage Boss

Familienweingut Tement 2021 Ried Zieregg Steilriegel GSTK Chardonnay (Südsteiermark)

Rounded and generous, this white offers a layered mix of ripe stone fruit, crisp orchard apples, and bright citrus, all framed by subtle baking spice and a touch of white peach. The texture is velvety and enveloping, while the lively acidity gives energy and structure. Mineral undertones and gentle salinity linger on the long, harmonious finish, making this a truly expressive and inviting wine. 93 Points — A.Z.

$100 Flatiron Wines

Outdoor Movies Call for Creative Wines

Whether you’re heading to a big screening at a park or hosting your own casual backyard affair, nothing beats slowly sipping on something delicious while the action unfolds.

“I like wines that would last the entire movie,” Zecevic says, “so nothing [too] glou-glou.” He recommends a “contemplative Barolo” but says that a complex white will also do the trick. 

If Barolo sounds a little heavy for a hot summer night, Capo suggests sticking to fun, light wines for easy drinking and pairing. “Screw caps and sparkling wines work great,” he says. “No corkscrew needed!”

Light and Lowkey

Aveleda 2025 Loureiro (Vinho Verde)

This wine substantially over-delivers at the price, as flavors of fresh apricot and green apple are accented on the nose by white pepper and river stone. It’s grounded by bright acidity and an underlying minerality, and finishes impressively long with excellent intensity. A great pairing for sushi, lighter chicken dishes, and the like. Drink over the next 2-3 years. Best Buy. 90 Points — John Sumners

$16 Total Wine

Piper Sonoma NV Blanc de Blancs Chardonnay (Sonoma County)

Aromas of juicy stone fruit, citrus, and lemon blossoms swirl on the nose of this gently effervescent sparkling wine. The palate delivers a pop of apple, ginger, and toasted almond flavors, with brioche and lemon pith dominating the finale. 90 Points — Tom R. Capo

$23 Piper Sonoma

Barolo

G D Vajra 2021 Albe Nebbiolo (Barolo)

This is Barolo’s soul in a glass, capturing the region’s magic through carefully blended vineyards. Wild cherries and crushed raspberries dance with fresh mint and bay leaf, while sweet and savory spices swirl beneath. The palate sparks with bright acidity, carrying crunchy red fruits, both fresh and dried, but always vibrant. Fine tannins lay down elegant rails for the wine to travel, showing why Barolo captures the world’s imagination. Drink Now – 2045. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — J.P.

$34 PJ Wine

Ratti winery 2021 Rocche Nebbiolo (Barolo)

This iconic cru is always emotive, wearing its heart on its sleeve, and in 2021, it radiates pure happiness. Bursting with energy, the wine leaps from the glass, proclaiming the vintage’s brilliance. Aromas of fancy hard cherry candies, drying roses, and sweet-savory spices enchant with vibrant intensity. The vineyard’s depth shines through darker fruit flavors, transitioning into earthy forest floor and grilled herbs. Tannins and acidity work in harmony, framing the wine with balance and ensuring its longevity. Both powerful and alluring, it promises to evolve beautifully. Drink from 2026. Cellar Selection. 97 Points — J.P.

$135 Plum Market

title: I Transitioned at the Peak of My Wine Career—and Wine Tastes Different Now
summary:
I spent the first 15 years of my career creating safe places for women to experience wine. I want to believe that deep down I knew I was also creating those spaces for me.

The post I Transitioned at the Peak of My Wine Career—and Wine Tastes Different Now appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
My palms were sweating as my heels clicked and clacked their way up the stairs to the convention center entrance. A cold gush of wind had my left hand instinctively reaching to secure my flirty yet professional dress, while my right brushed the long red hair away from my face. A woman held the door for me and smiled. I had just arrived in Monterey, California, to speak at the Direct to Consumer Wine Symposium, and I needed to grab my badge before the welcome happy hour. As I darted inside, it felt like a pit had opened in my stomach. 

I’ve spoken at dozens of wine industry events over the last 20 years, I’m friends with the board members, and my book, Rebel School of Wine, was just nominated for a James Beard Award. This should be easy. So why was I so nervous? Because the last time I attended this conference, I had a beard.

This was my first major wine industry event since I began my gender transition from male to female in 2023, and I wasn’t sure how people would respond to a transgender woman. It’s not like there are a lot of out trans women in this industry. In fact, the only other trans woman I knew of was Viviane Stardust Kennedy, the owner and winemaker of RAM Cellars in Oregon. We’re starting a club.

The lack of transgender women in powerful business roles kept me closeted for decades. I grew up in the ‘90s and the only transgender women I saw on TV were either disappointing their parents on Jerry Springer or in a body bag on Law and Order. I never saw a single transgender woman holding dignified jobs like doctors, lawyers, or what I decided I would be at 15 years old: a startup CEO. Boys who wore dresses weren’t trusted, and they most certainly weren’t allowed to be in charge of a real company. I had things I wanted to do with my life that I didn’t think I could do as a trans girl, so I put my head down, pretended to be a boy, and worked my butt off for the next 25 years. Then, one fateful day in 2023, I finally made the decision that would change my life forever.

Gender transitions are a wild ride. In two and a half years, thanks to a combination of hormone replacement therapy (HRT), hundreds of TikTok makeup tutorials, a wonderful personal stylist best friend, and lucky genetics, my appearance changed so much that even the TSA is confused and I do double takes when I look in the bathroom mirror. Acquaintances I haven’t seen in a while introduce themselves because they don’t recognize me.

HRT is a surprisingly simple cocktail of inexpensive, generic drugs that have been around for over 60 years. Spironolactone blocks testosterone, estradiol replaces it with estrogen, and the female sex hormone progesterone pours gasoline on the fire, pushing the body through a second puberty. The potential changes are well-documented, and I got all of them: softer skin, less body hair, breast tissue growth, significant loss of muscle mass, and body fat redistributed to the girl places. Emotions were the part that caught me off guard as I balled my eyes out watching a dog food commercial and found myself screaming at loved ones for no reason (I’m doing better now). I even noticed some niche changes: my fingers got smaller and colors became more vivid.

But HRT doesn’t do everything. I’m seeing a speech therapist to change my voice and after twelve painful sessions under the laser, my beard is gone. There are a few common surgeries, but I haven’t crossed that bridge yet.

Think of it like a computer. Our physical bodies are the hardware: the computer chips, hard drive, screen, etc. Hormones are like the software: the operating system and programs. I was born with a PC body and HRT is replacing Windows with MacOS. Our DNA already contains the blueprint for both sexes, and it’s incredible how our bodies just dust off the plans and get to work. The surgeries are like getting an external hard drive, a cute keyboard, and a colorful case. 

I thought I understood all the effects of HRT. If you own my comprehensive wine book, you know how thoroughly I research things. But 18 months into my transition, something changed that I didn’t expect; wine tasted very different.

My sense of smell increased by about 40%, and my sensitivity to bitter flavors became far more intense. What’s more, my mood and the setting are now critical. If I’m tired or stressed, wine just tastes kind of flat. I can still identify the flavors and aromas, but the overall experience has shifted. On the flip side, if I’m excited and I really connect with the wine’s story, it tastes ten times better. 

I was really worried about being a transgender woman in the wine industry, but nothing could have prepared me for being a woman in the wine industry.

The Bittersweet Science of Wine Tasting

To be fair, there had been some very obvious foreshadowing. Since 2007 I’ve been creating and operating wine event and experience companies, to great success. Wine Riot was a traveling wine festival I launched in 2009 that drew 150,000 attendees to 50 major events across the United States. The attention landed me on the cover of Wine Enthusiast, along with features in the Boston Globe and LA Times and a regular guest spot on the local morning news. In 2018, my next venture, Rosé Mansion, went viral and sold 225,000 tickets in one year. Sporting my pink suit and a beard, I casually gave walking tours of the space on Good Morning America, ABC News, and CNN. Almost all of the 375,000 people that came through my doors were women, as were my co-founder, Morgan First, and our entire staff. In fact, the Rosé Mansion audience was 98% women, and I quickly realized women taste and experience wine differently from men.

The user review data didn’t lie. Our Wine Riot events had a mobile app, which allowed tens of thousands of attendees to easily mark the wines they liked. At one of the New York events, I could see a handful of winery reps pointing at the “Top Wines” section of the giant TV screen that displayed the live mobile app data. I knew exactly what they were grumbling about before I could even hear their voices. The most popular wines at Wine Riot were always sweet wines.

“Everyone starts their wine journey drinking cheap sweet wines, but eventually you develop a real palate and learn to appreciate dry wines.” 

I’ve heard variations of this myth from hundreds of wine professionals over the years and, I will admit, I believed this early in my career. But it just didn’t match up with what occurred at my events. I saw women gravitating towards the sweeter wines and making sad puckering faces when someone poured them a dry red. These were intelligent, successful women, many with advanced degrees, and they purchased a lot of wine. (I have the data because we partnered with Wine Intelligence and surveyed attendees.) Far from being newbies with unsophisticated palates, these women were experienced drinkers who knew what they liked.

It all began making sense later that year when I met Tim Hanni, a Master of Wine who was doing academic research on taste perception and wine with a researcher at Cornell University. He explained that everyone is born with a certain sensitivity to bitter flavors that’s hard-coded into our DNA. The sensitivity is different for everybody, but women are generally much more sensitive than men.

At my events, women didn’t necessarily want sweet wines. Instead they wanted wines that didn’t taste bitter, and sugar is one way to mask those unpleasant flavors. Once I understood this, I decided to lean in!

At Rosé Mansion, the Pursuit of Sweetness room was my baby, and by far the most ambitious educational experience I had ever attempted. The 20 foot x 30 foot room had a custom vinyl floor that resembled a life-sized game of Candyland. Attendees were encouraged to follow the path leading to a series of questions printed on the floor. “How do you take your coffee or tea?” for example, had three potential answers: “lots of cream and sugar,” “a little cream and sugar,” or “black like my soul.” Based on the answer, the path split to the next question. “Do the tags in clothes bother you?” “Can you concentrate when the TV is on?” “How spicy do you like your tacos?” Eventually, the path led guests to one of three wines being poured at the bar labeled dry, semi-dry, or sweet.

My staff and I used peer-reviewed research papers studying the genes that determine bitterness sensitivity and the other markers, like itchy tags, and used it to create a game that would guess the style of wine our guests would prefer. Over the next few months,180,000 people played our little game. We were correct over 99% of the time.

But it wasn’t just a party trick. I was sick of hearing stories of women going to a fancy wine store and specifically asking for sweet wine, only to get home and find out they were given a dry Muscat wine instead. I was tired of women having to order cocktails at nice restaurants because the by-the-glass list only has dry wines. Most of all I was fed up with the looks on servers’ and sommeliers’ faces when someone asked for a sweet wine. Some of the greatest wines on the planet are sweet, and the same people who turned up their noses to sweet wines paired with a steak pick up a burger and a Coke after their shift. The goal of Pursuit of Sweetness was to arm women with the science and talking points to understand that their palates aren’t basic or unsophisticated and the language to always buy the wines they want.

Revisiting Black Coffee

I was not prepared for the changes to my own palate.

Last year, standing outside a gas station near Palm Springs, I choked and then spit out my first gulp of a cold brewed coffee. I checked the can to see what went wrong. I used to drink these 12 ounce cans of unsweetened Starbucks cold brew all the time. Now it tasted like chewing on a ball of black tar and old crusty leather mixed together, but it was actually the intensity that caused me to spit it out. For hours after that one sip, I couldn’t get the taste to go away. Eventually it dawned on me that I haven’t had one since I began taking hormones.

For over a decade I’ve understood the science behind taste perception and how aromas and flavors can vary significantly between men and women, but experiencing this variation first hand was a massive shock. I used to only drink black coffee, but in the months after I began HRT, I found myself adding a little bit of milk. Now I can’t drink coffee without a healthy pour and often I’ll add sugar just to make it palatable. I also crave sugar for the first time in my life, to the point that I’ve started keeping candy in the house.

My personal wine preferences have also shifted. I’ve always loved white wines and obviously drank a lot of rosés, but I find myself gravitating towards them more often, even during the cold New York winters, which previously was a season I reserved exclusively for red wines. I find myself less interested in very dry or tannic reds, even from top regions or producers. IPAs have become completely undrinkable. I ordered my first Negroni in a few years, which had been my drink of choice, but it tasted like bug spray mixed with Windex and I struggled to get it down. On the flip side, I crave light and bright red wines from California because now when I drink red wines, I want subtle yet deep and lasting flavors. Aromatic wines now envelop my entire head and I dream about the aromas for days after.

The technical aspect of wine tasting hasn’t changed at all. If anything, I’m a lot better at it because my sense of smell is far more acute. I can still pick out the same subtle aromas and flavors, just like before. It’s not like I’m a sweet wine drinker now, either; I just stray away from wines that taste a little bitter. Ultimately, it’s the experience of wine that changed the most. The people, the vibes, and my mood play a much larger role in my overall enjoyment of wine.

At this point, the science and research on taste perception is crystal clear. The exact same wine will provide a different tasting and flavor experience for each person, and hormones play a role in that difference. Obviously, that’s over-simplifying the subject, because there are additional fluctuations to a woman’s hormones after a pregnancy, during perimenopause, after menopause, and over a monthly cycle. There are mountains of research available. The large snack food, fast food, and soda conglomerates have been using this information to create all kinds of products for decades. So why isn’t this common knowledge in the U.S. wine industry?

What This Means for the Wine Industry

As U.S. wine sales plummet, the industry is searching for answers. There are many culprits, including inflation, tariffs, higher interest rates, the rise of GLP-1s, and consumers becoming more health-conscious. But I can’t even count how many times in my twenty-year career that a winery stakeholder told me, “We understand that young women are our future customers but old men are who buy our wines right now so we need to focus on them.” They may not have called them “old men,” but that’s always what I heard.

Women represent 59% of the consumers in the $115 billion U.S wine market, but the representation of women in decision making positions in the American wine industry is abysmal. Only 5% of wineries in California and Oregon are owned exclusively by women. At the 4,200 wineries in California only 14% have a woman as head winemaker, the position responsible for major decisions. Women fill only 16% of vice president-level or higher roles at large corporate wine companies and represent only 30% of Certified, Advanced and Master Sommeliers, the people most likely to be in charge of wine buying decisions at the best restaurants in the country.

Earlier this year I visited my friend, Shauna Rosenblum, who in 2022 became the head winemaker for Ridge Vineyards at Lytton Springs in Sonoma, California. She leads a team that makes over 50 different wines using both estate-grown vineyards and grapes they purchase. The property is best known for its Zinfandels, but Shauna decided to make some very small batches using unique white grapes she has access to, like Chenin Blanc. The tasting room team fell so in love with these new white wines they started hand-selling them to customers using only word of mouth and even created an ad hoc wine club. Within a year they were shipping hundreds of club orders for a wine club that didn’t even exist on the website. The Lights & Brights club is now online and growing rapidly. It includes gems like Chenin Blanc, Falanghina, and a blend of Palomino and Muscadelle grapes planted in 1922, and the bottles are engaging new customers. These wines are not made specifically for women. Rather, women simply had full control at every level of decision-making.

We see this happening in other industries, too. Cosmetics was traditionally a business where men created the products they wanted to see on women. Women purchased them because there weren’t any alternatives. Then, around 15 years ago, startup brands owned and operated by women like Anastasia, Glossier, and Rare Beauty started new trends like minimalist no-makeup makeup, shunning unrealistic beauty standards, and creating products inclusive for all skin tones. Sales shot through the roof and today these beauty startups are generating billions of dollars each year and they are growing three to four times faster than legacy brands.

A Critical Juncture

Back at the Direct to Consumer Wine Symposium, despite being terrified, I walked confidently into the convention center and picked up my badge. The next day I gave my presentation about how to market wine to Millennials and Gen Z for a room of 300 industry professionals. None of my fears materialized and, in fact, the opposite came true. People respected me for my industry knowledge and treated me with kindness. One week later I led a panel about design and branding at Unified Wine & Grape Symposium that got me featured in Forbes. Then in March I spoke at the Women for WineSense Grand Event in Seattle and was surprised with the Lifetime Achievement Award. I’ve also been asked to speak at the Women in Wine Festival in New York, FLXcurion in the Finger Lakes, and put on the keynote panel at the Blood Of Gods Annual Merrymaking in Walla Walla, Washington.

The current administration has made transgender women public enemy number two, after immigrants. As acts of anti-trans violence continue to rise as a result, safety is now my primary concern. I’m cautious because the wine industry has a lot of people that live in rural areas, who may not have much experience with transgender people. However, time and time again, the people in this industry who I’m worried will be critical of a transgender woman end up being the kindest, most trusted allies.

The U.S. wine industry does not have a great track record of treating women with the respect they deserve. But after spending the first six months of this year meeting and speaking with thousands of wine industry professionals, I firmly believe we are at a turning point. Not only is the American wine industry filled with so many good, well-meaning people who are actively trying to make the world a better place, but market factors are also forcing change. 

Wine has been a business on this planet for nine thousand years, and it will continue to be a business for nine thousand more. The U.S. wine industry is not going away, but there will be a drastic shift. It will no longer be optional to develop a true understanding of all wine consumers, including women. 

This fall I plan to open No Boys Allowed in New York, my first new wine experience since Rosé Mansion was forced to close in March of 2020 due to Covid restrictions. It’s a high-energy, extremely colorful, 10-seat, omakase-style wine experience meets one woman show led exclusively by yours truly. I spent the first 15 years of my career creating safe places for women to find joy while experiencing wine and be themselves. I want to believe that deep down I knew I was also creating those spaces for me.

For the transgender and nonbinary people in the wine industry, I am here to help you. Whether you’re out, closeted, or somewhere in between, let’s figure out how to make you shine. You don’t have to navigate the wine industry alone. Now you have me.

You can connect with Tyler Balliet via her personal Instagram @tylerballiet and she creates wine content via @rebelschoolofwine. Her book, Rebel School of Wine, is available at major bookstores or directly from her website: rebelschoolofwine.com.

Additional Transgender Resources

For a list of transgender organizations and resources: https://glaad.org/transgender/resources/

If you or someone you know are struggling please use these free resources. They will never report you or call the police.

Speakers: Maria Yagoa, Tyler Balliet

Transcript:

Maria Yagoda 

Well, it’s so great to finally meet you. You have had a front row seat, I feel like, to the wine industry’s journey over the past couple of decades, particularly with young people and millennials, and I’m curious, how you see the current discourse around Gen Z playing out. You know, it was ‘millennials are killing the wine industry,’ and now they’re the largest wine drinking cohort in America. Now Gen Z is killing the wine industry. What do you think is going on? 

Tyler Balliet

Yeah, so I got started throwing events for millennials, like I’m essentially the oldest millennial, and I built my entire career on building and creating these wine events and wine experiences for my friends, and everything is happening with Gen Z. They’re saying the exact same thing about, they said they said about millennials, like everything that they’re saying about Gen Z now, they said about millennials, and if you go back and you look into old newspapers, it’ll go back to like 1910 like the 1880s You always had these older, or yeah, these older generations complaining about the young people. They don’t do it like I did, they don’t consume things, they don’t go out, they don’t have the same habits that I had.

I think for wine it’s particularly difficult because there’s this giant disconnect between the people who make wine and the people who drink it, and it’s geographical, you know, like the people who make wine live in the country, they live in rural parts of the US. They live in rural Australia, and they’re not that well-connected to people that are in cities, which is where the most of the wine is consumed. So, there’s always been a disconnect. There’s always going to be a disconnect, but in the last 20 or 30 years, the internet had a dramatic change on everything in the world. I think we can all agree on that one, right? And as millennials, we were the first generation that was brought up with the internet. Like, I remember getting it in our classrooms. I remember the first time I went on the internet. Gen Z doesn’t remember that they’ve had an iPhone, since they were like eight or nine years old, and so they’re looking at the world differently, they’re seeing things differently, and they’re buying things and drinking and hanging out differently. Wine is slow to change, partially is because it takes so long to make the products, right, and you’re seeing a disconnect, and you’re seeing a shift in a change in the wine industry, and it’s not going to be good for some wineries, but there’s a lot that are figuring it out and making it work.

MY

I feel like you are very attuned to what’s working, or where the industry is falling short, and I feel like your personal journey over your career has especially given you insights into that.

TB

Yeah, I could say that, absolutely. So, you know, I started off working in wine when I was 25, 26 years old, and I didn’t come from a wine-making family, I came from Wisconsin, everybody drank Miller High Life, and I fell into wine, like a lot of people do later in life, I guess. So in New York, in Chicago, what you had was like restaurants, we have European wines, and then after World War Two, you had all these people that all these Americans that came back from Europe from the war with this taste for European wines, so they knew what these tasted like, and so Andre was like, we need to make these wines, we need to make dry wines, and so how they started separating it, and it was totally benign back then, was sweet wines were cheap and crappy, dry wines were high quality, and that was true back then. And that was the war, and they won it exactly 50 years ago this week. The judgment of Paris happened, and the California wines beat the French wines, and it was proof that California could make world class wines, they won the war on right, but the language didn’t stop, because at this point you’re now going on 5060 years of sweet wine, bad dry wine good, and the new crop of wine people that were coming up didn’t know why they didn’t know why sweet wine’s bad, dry wine’s good, because they’re too far distance from it. Whereas in the ‘70s they knew why, and they didn’t hate sweet wines, they just, that was words that they used to differentiate it, and they just kept pushing forward on this war on sweet wine, and they’ve continued and continued. What I think they don’t really know is it’s not a war on sweet wine anymore. It’s a war on women, it’s a war on people of color, and because it’s just they’re telling everybody that they’re wrong and that they shouldn’t drink these things, and they don’t even know why they’re doing it right, you know. And so they’re pushing away consumers.

MY

Oh my gosh, that is so interesting. I do not see people talking about that. That is a fascinating history, because I just have always accepted, oh, there’s this perception, but had no idea why. I’ve always had a sense in my bones that it was sort of sexist, that sweet wine is considered unsophisticated, and, but, but couldn’t really articulate or understand why, and I just,

TB

Yeah, it didn’t start out that way. It started out as a quality thing, and it was well understood, and it’s still thought to be a quality difference, but it isn’t anymore. And what it ended up becoming was this just pushing away consumers, but again, because the boomers and the baby boomers were drinking so much wine, and they were just being reintroduced to it, like I got into wine, because I went back to Wisconsin, like after I graduated college, and my entire family was drinking wine, I was like, what is happening here? These people were Miller High Life, forever, except my aunt, who drinks Miller Lite, and they all switched to wine, and I was like, I’m getting into wine, like this is a cultural shift that’s happening amongst the baby boomers, and so you had such this incredible growth with baby boomers that who cares that they pushed away women, wine sales went up every single year, regardless, and ever since the judge, whatever. Ever since the 60 Minutes Piece, the “French wine paradox.” So, ever since the French wine paradox in 60 minutes came out, I think in 1992 wine sales in America have been going up every single year until like two years ago. So, they’ve just been steadily increasing, so winery. If they do nothing in marketing, their wine sales are going to go up next year, right? There was just incredible growth, and that was due to a cultural shift and a cultural change. I gave a talk about how that’s like computer industry, right? In the 1990s like I’m old enough to remember going to Best Buy in 1996 and how they marketed computers, and it was like it’s a 46 processor with a 28 Bob modem and 16 megabyte hard drive. Do you want it? And nobody understood the computer jargon, but they bought computers anyways, not because Best Buy was good at marketing, but because the culture was changing. And then you had Steve Jobs come along and say it’s an iMac, you can watch movies and call your friends, and everyone’s just like, shut up and take my money, right? But he got rid of the jargon, and it was still there if you want to look for it, but he just removed the jargon, the industry jargon, you know, like computers was using the same language that engineers use to talk to each other, and they just turned into marketing copy, but that never works, it’s never worked ever, and that’s what the wine industry is doing now, and they’ve been using this and saying, well, but sales have been going up. But like, sales weren’t going up because you were good at this. Sales are going up because there was a cultural change. There was a cultural shift in America, and that caused sales to go up. And the proof is that they weren’t able to do anything when the sales started going down. They weren’t able to pull levers and shift that. You know,

MY

that’s fascinating. And so we’re kind of going through some of those growing pains, right? The industry is going through those that cultural shift right now, and I guess in the next few years, what do you think is happening and gonna happen? Do you think the industry is pivoting enough to keep this whole thing going?

TB

You know, the wine industry is not just one thing, you know. I think that that’s what makes it difficult. I am most closely connected to the wine producers in America, particularly California and Oregon and Washington, and so I’m most concerned about them, right? And they’re seeing, they’re seeing so many different shifts and changes all at once. It’s really, really difficult, you know. Wholesalers have just dropped them, like in New York, like find a California-centric wine list. I dare you. Like, I wish I could. Like, it’s all France, France, France, France, France, France, France. And a big part of that is because France spends $100 million a year in marketing and PR. They spend $100 million a year in marketing and PR, right? Every 10 years they pass a $1 billion budget for this. I with wine riot, like I lived off that money for a long time. The EU money, like I lived off that, like that’s how I paid my rent, is like the EU regions would come to us, we would build them booths, and like you know, so when Prohibition ended, the federal government said the deal was like, “We’ll let you have alcohol, but there won’t ever be a single dollar of federal money that ever goes to supporting alcohol, and so the US government spends $0 you know, and that’s an issue, and if you look at, look at the Got Milk campaign, right, the Got Milk campaign in the 90s and 2000 every single celebrity ever, and milk sales were going down, but and I’m from Wisconsin, so like I know, right, and so milk sales were going down, and it didn’t that campaign didn’t make stop them from going down, it just stopped them from going down as much, but they were in a unique position where you had all of these farmers, kind of like wine, where you have like 1000s of small wineries, right, and they had a couple like distributors, and those distributors said x percentage of this is going to this campaign, and if you don’t like it, we won’t buy your milk, right? So everybody had to pay in, it was a forced pan, and it really kept the industry alive, you know, like it’s a huge success. What is wine? If wine, wine has the ability to keep going. The market share is, it could be significantly more. The amount of money people are spending on wine right now is actually higher than it was last year. It was higher than it was the year before. The amount of money people are spending is going up. It’s not going to the California wineries, and the federal government’s not going to come in and save them. Banding together and pooling resources is the only thing that will, but it’s really hard to do that when you don’t have to. Like, how do you get the big players in this right? And you have the regions that kind of do it, but you have so many different regions that they don’t have that big of a budget. Like, there just needs to be a Got Mill campaign for wine hard stop. Yeah,

MY

Meanwhile, I can’t watch one minute of television without five Draft Kings commercials. I can’t watch two seconds of sports without, and the live odds, so that’s that’s a whole separate thing. Thing,

TB

The profit margins on casinos is really good, you know.

MY

Yeah. Oh my god.

TB

And wine doesn’t have a good profit margin, and that’s part of the issue too, is like there isn’t a lot of money in it, right? You know, like what, like a glass of, like a bottle of vodka. The vodka costs less money than the glass bottle, you know. And wine isn’t that thing. Wine is an agricultural product. It only comes out once a year. It’s a finite amount. It’s not saying I have solutions to the problem. I’m just saying that, like, it’s complicated, and you have the banks that are pulling funding from a lot of these wineries. You have insurance companies that are no longer insuring the farmland, and like the agriculture, it’s, it’s really, really tough when you don’t have an organization like the French government that can just come in and fund it, right? Like, we don’t have that, the state of California realizes that there’s tourism, and they throw money at it, right, but without coming together and having a national campaign that’s like this is wine, you know. Then you’re leaving it up to the kid at the wine bar to convince everybody that they should drink more wine, and I’m not exactly sure that that’s the best plan. And also, it’s not their job. The job of a sommelier is not to be an ambassador for wine, you know.

MY

Agreed, it’s too much, too much to ask of that kid,

TB

Also, you know, the French government’s gonna like spend a lot of money convincing them that the wines in France are the best, right?

MY

I mean, and elsewhere in Europe, you see wine being treated as this precious agricultural product, a cultural product that is so tied to national tradition, which you don’t really see so much here, and that could also be part of the problem, but yes,

TB

Wine isn’t even regulated by the Department of Agriculture, it’s related, regulated by the Department of Treasury, you know, because they can tax it. Yeah, the TTB is on, is in the Department of Treasury, you know, so that, so that’s who regulates grapes being grown in Napa Valley, that’s who regulates the wine regions and all that, it’s the Department of Treasury, and that was all based on prohibition, like, because they’re like, this is a thing, we’re gonna tax it. It was actually FDR, because FDR just gotten elected right before Prohibition ended, and he wanted to create a regional system of wine, like the US. They spent money on it. They actually built sample wineries where they would build schools, and they would build test government-owned test wineries in different regions around the US, so they could learn which grapes grew best, and then train people in that area to grow them, because they knew how much it stimulated the economy, they knew how much local products meant, and you know, France uses wine as this symbol of, like, French wine products are, or all French products are high quality, you know, like if you look at the big auction houses, like Sotheby’s and whatnot, like they know that if somebody comes to them and wants to sell $2 million worth of wine, they probably have $200 million worth of art. They’re not in it for the wine, they’re in it for the art, right. And so France isn’t in it for the wine, but they know that having everybody say French champagne is a celebration means French cheeses sell more, it means French cars sell more, it means French products are thought of as better, right? And so that’s why the French government invests in it.

MY

I love it, and so appreciate your time and sharing all of this with us. I guess to wrap it all up, June is Pride month. What are you feeling hopeful about in terms of the wine industry and the beverage industry at large, connecting with and embracing LGBTQ plus communities, you know, we’re living at a very scary, frightening time, and I’m gonna go off on my little thing. I mean, just a lot of, a lot of cowardice, a lot of bigger companies backing out of Pride celebrations. I have found it in my little space, the drinks community to be so welcoming and inclusive, and I, I’m wondering if what you’re looking forward to, to about June and Pride, if, if anything.

TB

Yeah, no, I love, I love this month now, like I said, I’m fairly new here, but I think with. Really makes me excited is every week or two I get a DM on Instagram from somebody who is like I’m studying wine, I’m non-binary, or like I’m transgender, and like seeing your content and seeing you, you know, talking and doing this just like gives me that’s what keeps me going, like I’m getting those messages, and I get so many of them now, and I spent 20 years too scared to come out. I spent 20 years pretending to be something I wasn’t, because I was scared to come out, because I didn’t think I could cut it in this industry. I didn’t think I would be accepted, and you will be, and you are, and you got me on your side, and I’m fighting for everybody, and it’s a really great industry of people that care, you know, like nobody’s in whining to make money, like none of us. We could all go to another industry and make money, but we love it here. We love the people, and I’m here because I love the people, and so if anybody’s out there that’s scared or nervous, like, send me a DM, message me, and it’s gonna be okay. And we’re gonna change the wine industry, you know. And if anybody wants to launch a got milk campaign for wine and wants, you know, a trans wine expert, like, I think I’m the girl for the job.

MY

Yes, I think this needs to happen. We’re going to start a movement when this, when this episode drops, it’s gonna.. I don’t know, I’ve just loved this conversation, and I’m just so appreciative of your time and perspective and energy. It’s so refreshing, honestly, because I think it is very easy to fall into the doom and gloom, and I certainly do, being on my phone all the time, but your energy is so infectious and precious that I just feel very lucky that I got to share with you.

TB

Thank you. I have nothing but hope and optimism. I, it’s, it’s gonna be bumpy, it’s gonna be rough, but we’re gonna get through it, you know.

title: Welsh Wine Would Like Your Attention, Please
summary:
After decades of disappointment, small-scale Welsh winemakers are working tirelessly to get noticed in a crowded wine world.

The post Welsh Wine Would Like Your Attention, Please appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
English wine has garnered ample attention and grown substantially in recent years. But it’s not the only country in the United Kingdom with a rapidly expanding wine industry. England’s neighbor to the west, Wales, is emerging as a serious wine-producing nation in its own right. A promising 2025 vintage—one many producers claim is the nation’s best yet—along with an increase in creative, natural-leaning producers, an expanding stack of awards, new winery facilities, and freshly awarded government support all point towards a bright future for Welsh wine.

Small yet Mighty

Let’s not get too carried away, though. Compared to England, which has nearly 12,000 acres planted to vines (as of 2025), Wales claims just over 230 acres. Over 1,100 vineyards are registered in England. Wales has just 60.

However, 60 is a whole lot more than six. That was the figure 16 years ago, one of the nation’s long-standing producers, White Castle Vineyard Proprietor Robb Merchant, told me in an email. one of the nation’s “The majority [of Welsh vineyards] are less than two hectares [or five acres],” he says.

These small vineyards are scattered around the edges of a nation that spans just over 8,000 square miles, a little smaller than New Jersey. Roughly 25% of Wales’s terrain is mountainous and therefore un-plantable (heck, unlivable). But these same ranges—namely Snowdonia in the north, the Cambrian Mountains in the middle, and the Brecon Beacons in the south—act as barriers for the wind and rain that thunders in off the Atlantic Ocean. Surrounded by water on three sides, Wales’s climate is indisputably maritime. But warm, dry pockets abound, if you know where to look for them. As wine writer and presenter Oz Clarke, member of Order of the British Empire (OBE), recently said in a presentation for Welsh Wine Week:

“[In Wales,] you can’t just pick a county, as some winemakers in England seem to do, and say, ‘Oh, that’ll be just right for a vineyard.’ You have to be far more specific.”

Welsh Wine: Then and Now

Vines have grown in fits and starts at some stage in Wales since the Roman Empire began. Throughout most of history their impact has been minimal. The climate never got warm enough for grapes to ripen consistently. 

Wales’s modern wine industry technically began in the 1960s. But it took the better part of a half century to move beyond amateur endeavors. Only in the last 25 years has the nation’s wine scene risen to a level of professionalism worthy of global recognition. This, in large part, is due to improved viticultural knowledge—a better understanding of what kind of grapes grow well where.

Today, over two dozen wine grape varieties grow as far north as Anglesey, an island off the northwest coast. But, as in England, wine grapes seem happiest in the Southeast, particularly in Monmouthshire, home to the majority of Welsh vineyards. This part of Wales can get as many sunshine hours as England’s sunniest counties of Essex and Kent, allowing not just the cold-hardy German hybrids created in the mid-20th century such as Rondo, Regent, and Ortega to thrive, but vinifera too. Chardonnay and Pinot Noir lead the pack. Albariño makes an appearance, particularly at biodynamic estate, Ancre Hill; and Wales’s first 1,000 vines of Cabernet Franc were planted by Merchant at White Castle in 2019.

Stylistically speaking, Welsh wine isn’t drastically different from its English brethren.

“It’s not as marked as you might think,” says British wine writer Susy Atkins. “The climate in south Wales is broadly similar [to England’s]; a bit wetter and windier, though.” Vintages variation is, therefore, more pronounced. “Indeed, it is a real rollercoaster in Wales!”

One grape that does set Wales apart from its eastern neighbor is the white hybrid variety, Solaris. Atkins thinks it stands out as “interesting” due to its bright-fruited, high-acid, easy-drinking nature. At the 2024 Welsh Wine Awards, it was a Solaris from Montgomery Vineyard—described by Atkins as having “plenty of citrus, pineapple and a hint of honeysuckle”—that took home the award for Best Still Wine.

Natty Wines Take Hold

Even more distinguishing than Solaris is Wales’s thriving natural wine scene, one that has gained momentum in recent years thanks in large part to foundations laid by Richard Morris, proprietor of the previously mentioned Ancre Hill.

In 2006, Morris and his wife, Joy, planted 500 Chardonnay and Albariño vines on their property in the Wye Valley in Monmouthshire, on the southeast border with England. Eight years later, they’d expanded to 30,000 vines and added Pinot Noir, Albariño, and other varieties to the mix. They certified the entirety of their vineyards organic and biodynamic, and built a highly sustainable winery made entirely from natural materials like straw bales, lime mortar, and sedum grass. 

Today, Ancre Hill leads the charge for biodynamic farming and low-intervention winemaking in Great Britain. The range of wines made by the estate’s longtime winemaker Jean du Plessis includes an electric-hued “Orange Wine” with a distinctive Clockwork Orange label made from a blend of Chardonnay, Albariño, Solaris, and Ortega. It’s gained somewhat of a cult following in Britain. “We were the first U.K. producer of orange wine on a commercial scale,” says Morris. There’re colorful pé-nats and seriously classy traditional method sparklers, too.

While Ancre Hill remains Wales’s sole biodynamic certified vineyard, organic and regeneratively farmed vineyards—stewarded by new producers inspired by Morris’s lo-fi approach—are emerging.

One of those producers is Morris’s son David, whom Richard recently sold one of his vineyards to. Along with his own brand, Mountain People, David makes wine for other small brands in the region at his family’s winery, making him Wales’s first contract winemaker.

Paul Rolt and Jemma Vickers of Hebron Vineyard and Winery, located in West Wales in Carmarthenshire county, are two of David’s former clients. The pair moved to Wales in 2010 after nine years establishing an organic vineyard in Andalusia, Spain. David, who shares their “zero zero” approach to winemaking, made wine for Hebron until 2024 when Rolt and Vickers opened their own winery. Today, Hebron’s wines are made from Solaris and Rondo, all fermented in ceramic “tinajas” or amphorae. Rolt is taking the nation’s passion for natural wine to another level.

“I am the lead applicant to protect the name ‘Natural Wine of Wales’ under the Geographical Indications scheme,” he says. Rolt applied in 2021 and says his application is “still progressing with the U.K. government,” but his hope is that by legally defining the term, it will protect natural producers “from brand washing and misinformation”.

Headwinds Abound

Rolt’s concerns with his industry’s future lie not just in misinformation surrounding natural wine marketing, but in the fact that Welsh wines are frequently made outside Wales, and even occasionally blended with non-Welsh fruit.

“Only 10% of vineyards in Wales actually process and vinify in Wales. The vast majority of Welsh wine is made in two wineries across the national border in England,” Rolt says. “If we are to establish Wales as a global wine region, then, of course, we should be working in Welsh wineries with Welsh-grown grapes.”

While warming temperatures benefit those grapes, which ripen easier with heat, Wales also has a unique set of climatic hurdles to contend with. Its exposed location at the edge of an island, perched in the North Atlantic means that cold winds—sometimes as low as 14℉—collide with budding vines in early springtime. Winds in late summer can reach as high as 80 miles per hour, battering the vines. There can be two months straight of 100% cloud cover during half of the growing season. And new rainfall records continue to be set, year after year. “Our biggest challenge is climate change,” says Rolt. “I cannot mitigate that.”

Despite the challenges, Welsh wine is well-placed for success thanks to a growing number of producers ensuring its place in the nation’s future.

“Welsh wine is having a moment of good reviews and recognition,” says Merchant. “The growers are small scale and therefore give their vines and grapes more attention. This allows for producers to seek their own individuality.” 

This individualism is helping the entire category to carve out its own space in a now-crowded wine world. It’s a space we should all be watching.

More Wine Coverage

  • A wellness resort where wine and cocktails are part of the program? Yes, you read that right.
  • It’s officially rosé season, and we rounded up our favorites from around the world.
  • More of a white wine drinker? From light and fresh to full and complex, Gavi does it all.
  • Director of Digital Product Jessyca Frederick visited Wine Enthusiast HQ to learn how to calibrate wine like our writers at large.
  • Beyond red drink, wine pros share the wines they’re reaching for to celebrate Juneteenth.
