title: How Our First 100-Point Spirit Earned Its Perfect Score
summary:
Cazcanes’ Destilado de Agave Nuestras Raices Still Strength Blanco stands out for its impressive blend of purity and complexity.

The post How Our First 100-Point Spirit Earned Its Perfect Score appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
It may be hard to believe, but this tequila is the first 100-point spirit I’ve ever published in 15 years of reviewing spirits for Wine Enthusiast. 

To be fair, it’s not the first 100-pointer I’ve assigned. Early in my tenure, a Japanese whisky earned a perfect score. But when it became clear that the whisky was a limited allocation that had already sold out in the United States, we yanked it from the lineup, and the review never ran. The magic happened again a few years ago, with a delightful Armagnac… that turned out to be not imported to the U.S. So that review never published, either.

Starting this year, Wine Enthusiast does review wine and spirits that are not imported into the U.S. But it also just so happened that one bottle that is available Stateside did get a perfect score. And I’m not pulling any punches this time.

In a recent blind tasting of blanco tequilas, Cazcanes Destilado de Agave Nuestras Raices Still Strength Blanco stood out as remarkably expressive and beautifully untamed in a way that few spirits can be.

Let me break it down: This is a destilado de agave (agave distillate), meaning it’s not certified as tequila, though it has all the hallmarks and was tasted alongside blanco tequilas. While Cazcanes makes several tequila expressions, including still-strength blancos, this distillate was made at a remote location, away from Cazcanes’ usual distillery, which was not certified by Mexico regulators. Opting to release a non-certified bottling was a deliberate choice, and one I’m glad was made.

The liveliness and complexity of the liquid is what made it so memorable. Every sip seemed to taste a little different—by turns citrusy, vegetal, grassy, and lightly spiced.

Yet, it wasn’t flashy. It had depth. It enticed, rather than hit you over the head (or the palate, so to speak). Usually, “still strength” is code for overproof spirits, of which I’m generally not a fan. This one, bottled at 47% (most tequilas are bottled around 40%), seemed worthy of the extra proof, resulting in bold flavor, not fire.

Founded in 2018, Cazcanes is a relatively small operation; its name refers to the Cazcan people, an indigenous group in Mexico. Humberto Alvarado, a third-generation master distiller, is cited as the original inspiration for the brand, after he shared rustic, ancestral-style distillate with the founders.

He doesn’t make the standard-issue Cazcanes—that would be master distiller Francisco “Don Chico” Jimenez Lazcarro, who has made some Cazcanes bottlings that have also scored well. 

But Don Chico isn’t the author of this particular 100-point bottling. For that, credit is due to Alvarado, who created this agave distillate in a small taberna in the jungles of Jalisco. The company’s nickname for the liquid is “Jugo de la Selva,” i.e. jungle juice. I prefer to go with Alvarado’s name for the small-batch distillate: Nuestras Raices, which is Spanish for “our roots.” Whatever you call it, it’s phenomenally expressive, agave-forward with tons of bell pepper and jalapeño, lime peel, and a surprising hint of cacao tumbling into an extraordinarily long finish showing crumbled sage, ginger root, and a final flicker of cayenne.

Of note, Cazcanes might not be a small brand for long. In late 2025, Jeff Kozak, previously CEO of Vermont’s Whistlepig whiskey, signed on as president of Cazcanes, and brought in execs formerly with Bacardi and Samson & Surrey. I’ll be keeping an eye on what happens next. Here’s hoping it leads to more bottlings like this one.

Cazcanes Destilado de Agave Nuestras Raices Still Strength Blanco

Note: this is not certified as a tequila nor mezcal, it’s a destilado de agave (agave distillate). Nomenclature be damned – this is an exquisite spirit. It’s downright kaleidoscopic, by turns citrusy, vegetal, lightly spiced, ever-changing from one sip to the next. But if it must be pinned down: expect a zesty aroma and a lively, complex, bold flavor. Tons of bell pepper and jalapeno, lime, and surprising hint of cacao give way to an extraordinarily long finish showing crumbled sage, slivers of lime peel, and ginger root, extinguished by a final cayenne flicker. This is one to sip and share. 2025 Special Edition. Nuestras Raices means “our roots.” 100 Points — Kara Newman
